title: Java Interview Questions & Answers (Q129-160)
description: Detailed answers for Java interview questions 129-160 covering JPMorgan questions, Spring Boot basics, and foundational concepts

questions.question: How can we make an existing HashMap thread-safe without using ConcurrentHashMap?
How do you ensure consistent data across multiple application instances? For example, if two JVM instances are running and a value is updated in App1 (JVM1), how will it be reflected in App2 (JVM2)?
Can we use @Transactional on protected or private methods?
What is the internal implementation of the @Transactional annotation?
Your application is deployed to production and experiencing high traffic. How would you handle it? What scaling strategies would you use?
How do you get alerts for 500 Internal Server Errors occurring every 5 minutes?
How do you handle partial failures in microservices architecture?
How would you log all methods annotated with @Transactional? (Provide pseudo code.)

If your Kafka-based application is deployed with multiple consumer instances, how do you ensure that only one consumer instance processes messages from a topic? Which configuration/property would you use?
How do you handle failures during Kafka message consumption in microservices?
How do you handle duplicate payments if a customer accidentally clicks the 'Pay' button multiple times? How do you identify and inform the client about duplicates?
How do you achieve zero data loss in your application?
What is Spring and Spring Boot? What are the advantages of Spring Boot?
questions.answer:
Spring is a comprehensive Java framework for building enterprise applications. Spring Boot is a Spring project that simplifies Spring application development.

**Spring Framework**:
Spring is built on core principles: Dependency Injection (DI), Inversion of Control (IoC), and Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP). It provides modular components for different layers:
- Spring Core: IoC container and DI
- Spring Data: Database abstraction
- Spring Security: Authentication and authorization
- Spring Web: REST API and web layer

**Spring Boot**:
Spring Boot is an opinionated, production-ready framework built on Spring that aims to make it easier to build stand-alone, production-grade Spring-based applications.

**Key Advantages of Spring Boot**:

1. **Auto-Configuration**:
```java
// Spring Boot automatically configures beans based on classpath
@SpringBootApplication
public class MyApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args);
    }
}
// No XML configuration needed!
```

2. **Embedded Server**:
```java
// Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow embedded - no WAR files needed
// Application runs as standalone JAR
java -jar application.jar
```

3. **Convention over Configuration**:
```yaml
# application.yml - Sensible defaults
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/mydb
    username: root
    password: secret
  # Other properties auto-configured based on type
```

4. **Starter Dependencies**:
```xml
<!-- Instead of managing many versions -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>
<!-- Automatically includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, Jackson, etc. -->
```

5. **Production-Ready Features**:
```java
@Component
public class HealthCheck extends AbstractHealthIndicator {
    @Override
    protected void doHealthCheck(Health.Builder builder) {
        // Custom health checks
        builder.up().withDetail("database", "connected");
    }
}

// Actuator endpoints auto-enabled
// /actuator/health, /actuator/metrics, etc.
```

6. **Easy Testing**:
```java
@SpringBootTest
public class UserServiceTest {
    @MockBean
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Autowired
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    public void testGetUser() {
        // Full Spring context loaded for testing
    }
}
```

7. **Logging Integration**:
```yaml
logging:
  level:
    root: INFO
    com.myapp: DEBUG
  pattern:
    console: "%d{HH:mm:ss.SSS} [%thread] %-5level %logger{36} - %msg%n"
```

**Comparison: Spring vs Spring Boot**

Spring:
- Requires extensive XML configuration
- Manual dependency management
- Need to deploy WAR to server
- Complex setup for new projects

Spring Boot:
- Minimal/no configuration
- Auto-managed dependencies
- Run as standalone JAR
- Quick setup and deployment

**Spring Boot Application Structure**:
```
src/
├── main/
│   ├── java/
│   │   └── com/myapp/
│   │       ├── Application.java (main class)
│   │       ├── controller/
│   │       ├── service/
│   │       ├── repository/
│   │       └── entity/
│   └── resources/
│       └── application.yml
└── test/
```

**Core Dependencies Spring Boot Provides**:
- Spring Framework Core
- Spring Security
- Spring Data JPA
- Jackson for JSON
- Logging via SLF4J
- Unit testing with JUnit and Mockito

questions.question: What are Spring Boot starters?
questions.answer:
Spring Boot Starters are dependency descriptors that simplify Maven/Gradle configuration. They provide transitive dependencies that work together.

**What They Do**:
Instead of specifying all individual dependencies, a single starter brings in all necessary libraries for a feature.

**Example: Spring Boot Web Starter**:
```xml
<!-- Without starter - needs to list all -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-webmvc</artifactId>
    <version>5.3.0</version>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-tomcat</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
    <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- With starter - simple! -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>
```

**Common Spring Boot Starters**:
```xml
<!-- Web Applications -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- Data Access -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- Security -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-security</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- Testing -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>

<!-- Messaging -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-amqp</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- Actuator/Monitoring -->
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-actuator</artifactId>
</dependency>
```

**What Gets Included**:
```
spring-boot-starter-web includes:
├── spring-webmvc
├── spring-boot-starter-tomcat
├── jackson-databind
├── validation-api
└── spring-boot-starter-logging

All compatible, tested versions automatically managed
```

**Custom Starter Creation**:
```java
// Create custom starter for your organization
// my-company-starter/pom.xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <!-- Add your organization's common libraries -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.mycompany</groupId>
        <artifactId>common-utils</artifactId>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

// In your projects
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.mycompany</groupId>
    <artifactId>my-company-starter</artifactId>
</dependency>
```

**Key Benefits**:
1. Simplified POM/build files
2. Compatible version management
3. Reduces configuration errors
4. Faster project setup
5. Industry best practices built-in

questions.question: What is the default port number of a Spring Boot application?
questions.answer:
The default port number for a Spring Boot application is **8080**.

**Changing Default Port**:

```yaml
# application.yml
server:
  port: 9090
```

```properties
# application.properties
server.port=9090
```

```java
// Programmatically
@Configuration
public class ServerConfig implements WebServerFactoryCustomizer<TomcatServletWebServerFactory> {
    @Override
    public void customize(TomcatServletWebServerFactory factory) {
        factory.setPort(9090);
    }
}
```

**Environment-Specific Ports**:
```yaml
# application-dev.yml
server:
  port: 8080

# application-prod.yml
server:
  port: 8443
  ssl:
    key-store: classpath:keystore.jks
    key-store-password: secret
```

**Dynamic Port Assignment**:
```java
@Configuration
public class DynamicPortConfig {
    @Bean
    public EmbeddedServletContainerCustomizer containerCustomizer() {
        return container -> container.setPort(0);  // OS assigns random port
    }
}
```

**Using Environment Variable**:
```bash
export SERVER_PORT=3000
java -jar application.jar
```

**Command-Line Override**:
```bash
java -jar application.jar --server.port=5000
```

The default port 8080 can be easily overridden through multiple methods depending on your deployment needs.
